textInfo.title: High ARA/EPA Ratio
textInfo.medicalDescription: ARA/EPA Ratio: 25.3, which is significantly higher than the reference range of 2.0-10.8. A high ratio indicates a pro-inflammatory state.
textInfo.originalDescription: ARA/EPA Ratio: 25.3
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your ARA/EPA ratio is 25.3, which is much higher than the recommended range of 2.0-10.8. This ratio compares a pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid (ARA) to an anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acid (EPA). A high value suggests a pro-inflammatory balance, which can be improved by increasing EPA intake.

textInfo.title: Low Phosphate Level
textInfo.originalDescription: Phosphat: 0.76 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your phosphate level is 0.76 mmol/l, which is below the normal range of 0.87-1.45 mmol/l. Phosphate is essential for bone health, energy production, and nerve function. Low levels can occur for various reasons and may warrant further investigation.

textInfo.title: Normal position and uterine lining
textInfo.medicalDescription: Anteflexio-anteversio. Normale Breite des Endometriums

textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows the position and lining of your uterus. The term 'anteflexion-anteversion' describes the normal forward tilt of your uterus within the pelvis. The examination also confirmed that the endometrium, which is the inner lining of the uterus, has a normal thickness. This is a standard and healthy finding.

Keine aktivierten Veränderungen der

textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your spine MRI shows that the endplates, which are the top and bottom surfaces of your vertebrae that connect to the discs, have no signs of active inflammation or damage. The spaces for the discs themselves appear unremarkable. The report mentions a possibility of very minimal disc wear, called discopathy, at the levels of C5/C6 and C6/C7 in your neck. This is considered a very subtle finding, suggesting the earliest stages of age-related changes without significant degeneration or nerve involvement.

textInfo.title: High Omega-6 to Omega-3 Ratio
textInfo.medicalDescription: Omega-6/Omega-3 Ratio (6/3): 8.8, which is above the reference range of 2.0-7.5. The report indicates this imbalance can have significant short- and long-term clinical effects. A high ratio is linked to increased risk of various inflammatory conditions and cardiovascular diseases.
textInfo.originalDescription: Omega-6/Omega-3 Ratio (6/3): 8.8
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your ratio of omega-6 to omega-3 fatty acids is 8.8, which is higher than the ideal range of 2.0-7.5. A high ratio suggests an imbalance that may promote inflammation. To improve this ratio, you can increase your intake of omega-3s (from fatty fish, flax, chia) and potentially reduce sources of omega-6s (like certain vegetable oils).

textInfo.title: Benign bone lesion in the left lower leg
textInfo.medicalDescription:
Scharf begrenzte, ovaläre,
ca. 2 cm messende Läsion in der proximalen Fibula. Interpretation: Nachweis einer ca. 2 cm messenden Läsion in der proximalen Fibula links,
DD Enchondromen – projektionsradiografische Korrelation (falls noch nicht bekannt) empfohlen.
textInfo.originalDescription:
Scharf begrenzte, ovaläre,
ca. 2 cm messende Läsion in der proximalen Fibula
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI detected a well-defined, oval-shaped lesion measuring approximately 2 cm in the upper part of your left fibula (the smaller of the two lower leg bones). Based on its appearance, the radiologist suggests this is likely a benign (non-cancerous) cartilage tumor called an enchondroma. To confirm this finding, especially if it has not been seen before, the radiologist recommends a follow-up with a standard X-ray of the area for further evaluation.

textInfo.title: Benign cysts on the cervix (Nabothian cysts)
textInfo.medicalDescription: cluster of high-signal fluid-filled sacs within the cervical stroma is characteristic of Nabothian cysts
textInfo.originalDescription: cluster of high-signal fluid-filled sacs within the cervical stroma is characteristic of Nabothian cysts
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a cluster of what are known as Nabothian cysts on your cervix. These are small, harmless, fluid-filled sacs that form when mucus-producing glands on the surface of the cervix become covered by normal skin cells, trapping the mucus inside. They appear as bright spots on the MRI scan due to their high fluid content. This is a very common and benign finding, similar to a blocked pore on the skin, and does not require any follow-up.

textInfo.title: Superficial cartilage wear on the right kneecap
textInfo.medicalDescription:
Oberflächliche Knorpeldefekte an der lateralseitigen Rückfläche der
Patella.
textInfo.originalDescription:
Oberflächliche Knorpeldefekte an der lateralseitigen Rückfläche der
Patella.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI reveals superficial cartilage defects on the back surface of your right kneecap (patella). This means there are areas of minor, surface-level wear on the smooth cartilage that allows your kneecap to glide smoothly as you bend your knee. These particular defects are located on the outer side of the kneecap's rear surface. This finding indicates early signs of cartilage wear.

textInfo.title: Low Docosahexaenoic Acid (DHA)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Docosahexaensäure C22:6 n-3 (DHA): 94 μmol/l, converted to 30.88 mg/l. This value is below the reference range of 105-290 μmol/l. The report indicates this is a low value. DHA is an essential component of the brain and retina. Deficiency is associated with some forms of depression and cognitive impairment.
textInfo.originalDescription: Docosahexaensäure C22:6 n-3 (DHA): 94 µmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your level of Docosahexaenoic Acid (DHA), a crucial omega-3 fatty acid for brain and eye health, is below the normal range. Low DHA levels have been linked to cognitive issues. To increase your DHA, consider consuming fatty fish or algae-based supplements.

textInfo.title: Normal findings in both hip joints
textInfo.medicalDescription: Erhaltene Gelenkstellung. Kein Gelenkerguss. Kein Knochenmarködem.Geringe Störung der Taillierung am anterioren/superioren Übergang Femurkopf/Femurhals. Interpretation: Hüften bds. unauffällig, insbesondere kein Knochenmarködem, intaktes Labrum und keine Knorpeldefekte.
textInfo.originalDescription:
Erhaltene Gelenkstellung. Kein Gelenkerguss. Kein Knochenmarködem.Geringe Störung der Taillierung am
anterioren/superioren Übergang Femurkopf/Femurhals.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI scan of both hip joints shows that they are unremarkable and healthy. The alignment of the joints is normal, and there is no sign of excess fluid (joint effusion) or bone bruising (bone marrow edema). The labrum, a ring of cartilage that helps stabilize the hip, is intact, and the protective cartilage covering the bones shows no defects. The radiologist noted a minor variation in the shape at the transition between the head and neck of your thigh bone, but this is considered a normal anatomical variance and is not a cause for concern.

textInfo.title: Low Eicosapentaenoic Acid (EPA)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Eicosapentaensäure C20:5 n-3 (EPA): 15.3 μmol/l, converted to 4.63 mg/l. The value is below the reference range of 18.0-140.0 μmol/l. The report notes this as a low value. EPA offers protection against atherothrombosis and is correlated with a lower incidence of coronary events.
textInfo.originalDescription: Eicosapentaensäure C20:5 n-3 (EPA): 15.3 µmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your level of Eicosapentaenoic Acid (EPA), an important omega-3 fatty acid, is below the normal range. EPA is known for its cardiovascular benefits, including protecting against plaque buildup in arteries. Fatty fish like salmon, herring, and mackerel are excellent sources of EPA.

textInfo.title: Localized cartilage damage in the right knee
textInfo.medicalDescription: Fokaler Knorpeldefekt an der posterolateralen Femurkondyle.
textInfo.originalDescription: Fokaler Knorpeldefekt an der posterolateralen Femurkondyle.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a focal cartilage defect in your right knee. This means there is a specific, localized area of damage to the smooth, protective cartilage that covers the end of your thigh bone (femur). The defect is located on the outer, back part of the bone within the knee joint (posterolateral femoral condyle). Cartilage acts as a cushion in the joint, and a defect can represent an area of wear or injury.

textInfo.title: High Ferritin Level
textInfo.originalDescription: Ferritin (Siemens): 296.0 µg/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your ferritin level, which reflects the amount of iron stored in your body, is higher than the normal range of 30-200 µg/l. High ferritin can indicate iron overload or inflammation. Further evaluation may be needed to determine the cause.

textInfo.title: Elevated Fat in Right Psoas Major Muscle
textInfo.medicalDescription: The intramuscular fat (IMF) of the right psoas major muscle is 14.4%, which is above the normal range of 4.5% - 7.1%.
textInfo.originalDescription: biomarker_name: IMF_psoas_major right, value: 14.4, unit: %, minNormalValue: 4.5, maxNormalValue: 7.1
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The analysis of your back muscles shows that the amount of fat within your right psoas major muscle is 14.4%. This is considered intermediate as it is above the typical range of 4.5% to 7.1% for individuals of your demographic. Higher levels of intramuscular fat can be associated with reduced muscle quality and function.

textInfo.title: Low Omega-3 Index
textInfo.medicalDescription: Omega-3 Index (INDO3): 2.7%, which is in the unfavorable range (<4.0%). A higher index (>8.0%) is considered protective for cardiovascular health.
textInfo.originalDescription: Omega-3 Index (INDO3): 2.7 %
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Omega-3 Index is 2.7%, which is considered unfavorable (<4.0%). This index measures the percentage of EPA and DHA in your red blood cells and is a marker of your long-term omega-3 status. A higher index (>8%) is associated with better cardiovascular health.
